figures.caption: Table 4: Relative errors (%) as ∆t varies in boundary tests. Parametric estimators: mean ˘ std in 10 trials (M“2,000 subsampled from Mtotal“20,000). Green = best among unlabeled methods.
Table 5: Condition number κpA˚q across all models. Rate in N : log-log regression Blog κ{BlogN ; Proposition B.3 predicts slope ď 1.

Figure 4: Condition numbers scaling with N for the normal matrix and its diagonal blocks (κ˚, κV V , κΦΦ). Slopes (italic numbers at N“100) are log-log regression rates from Table 7. Dashed/dotted green lines show the OpNq theoretical rates. Left: κ˚ increases in N , with a rate near OpNq when d “ 10. Center: κV V is N -independent. Right: κΦΦ has a rate near OpNq when d “ 10 due to distance concentration.

Figure 3: Convergence for discrete-time model (i.e., δt “ ∆t, zero gap). The intrinsic Op∆tq bias of the discrete-time model (see Section 2.3) dominates the error when ∆t is large, and the convergence in M is only visible at ∆t ď 10´3 (and ∆t “ 10´4 for Φ) where the bias is small enough to allow the OpM´1{2q statistical error to dominate. See also in Table 6.

Table 7: Condition numbers of the normal matrix and its diagonal blocks in self-test LSE for the reference model. Rate in N is estimated via log-log regression B log κ{B logN ; Proposition B.3 predicts that κ˚ “ OpNq, i.e. a rate 1.

Figure 7: Typical estimators of V 1 and Φ1 for the radial potentials when d “ 2. Top left: Smoothness test. Top right: Conditioning test. Bottom left: Singularity test (LJ). Bottom right: Smooth control (Morse). Gray shading indicates the empirical density of observed distances.

Table 2: Method comparison across observation intervals ∆t for the reference model (relative gradient errors, %). Oracle: true basis, mean ˘ std over 10 trials. NN: MLP r64,64,64s, mean ˘ std over 10 trials. Green = best per ∆t.

Figure 1: Workflow of both estimation algorithms using the self-test loss function. Left: Least squares regression expands V and Φ in prescribed basis functions (or RBFs), and solves the minimizer via least squares with regularization. Right: Neural network regression parameterizes Vθ and Φθ by neural networks, computes derivatives via automatic differentiation, and minimizes the loss using stochastic gradient descent.
Figure 5: Non-radial potential recovery (d“2). Percentages are relative L2pρq errors.

Table 6: M -scaling of self-test LSE: relative L2pρq errors (%) for the reference model (d“2, N“10, 10 trials per cell). (a)-(b): Riemann sum and Trapezoidal quadrature for time integration. The observation time step ∆t P t10´3, 10´2, 10´1u, and the data is generated with time step fixed at δt “ 10´4. (c): Zero-gap regime (δt “ ∆t). The slope is estimated by log-log regression of error vs. M . Those slopes matching the theoretical OpM´1{2q rate are highlighted in green. See Figure 2 for the corresponding plots.

Figure 6: Typical estimators of Φ1prq for the four boundary test models (d“2, M“2,000, σ“1, δt“10´4, ∆t“10´2). Gray shading indicates the approximate exploration measure ρΦ. Smoothness: Smoothed indicator with sharp transitions at r “ 0.5 and r “ 1. Conditioning: Slowdecaying 1{pr`1q interaction that challenges conditioning. Singularity: Lennard-Jones r´12´r´6 singularity; gradient diverges as r Ñ 0. Smooth control: Morse potential with equilibrium at r0 “ 0.8.

Figure 2: M -scaling on the reference model under Riemann-sum (left pair) and trapezoidal (right pair) time integrations. Both with data generated with δt “ 10´4 and integrated with the various ∆t values. The Riemann-sum has error bound Op∆t ` M´1{2q, so the four ∆t values track the OpM´1{2q rate (green line) until they saturate at an Op∆tq floor (left pair), while the trapezoidal rule has error bound Opp∆tq2 ` M´1{2q, so all four ∆t values track the OpM´1{2q rate without saturation (right pair). The mean and standard deviation are computed over 10 trials per point; detailed numbers in Table 6.
